text: We present the case of a thirty five year old female with a history of Crohn's disease and failed medical management. She presented with left sided Crohn's colitis confirmed on CT imaging and colonoscopy findings. The diagram illustrates the setup and port placement for the case.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the diseased left colon using a lateral to medial approach, progressing towards the splenic flexure. Care is taken to avoid excessive retraction on the spleen to prevent capsular tears. The distal transverse colon is mobilized, and the greater omentum is dissected to enter the lesser sac, continuing dissection towards the splenic flexure to connect with the previous left colon mobilization.

With the left colon fully mobilized from the retroperitoneum, the surgeon dissects and skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), using an endoscopic stapler to ligate it. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is then dissected and ligated with an endoscopic stapler, taking care to avoid the left ureter. The middle colic vessels are dissected and ligated with an endoscopic stapler, requiring a twelve millimeter port in the left upper quadrant for this step.

The surgeon mobilizes the right colon using a supracolic approach, advancing the dissection towards the hepatic flexure. The duodenum and head of the pancreas are visible, with care taken to avoid injury. The dissection continues in a lateral to medial infracolic approach, fully mobilizing the right colon. The ileocolic pedicle is dissected, and an endoscopic stapler is used to ligate the ileocolic vessels.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the rectum by opening the peritoneal reflection on the right and left lateral aspects of the upper rectum. The rectum is mobilized circumferentially in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, with careful anterior dissection to avoid injuring the vagina. The posterior rectal dissection progresses towards the pelvic floor.

The surgeon uses gentle pushing to reveal tissue planes before cutting during the dissection. The intra-abdominal dissection of the rectum reaches its distal limit, and the perineal dissection begins via the intersphincteric plane to enter the pelvic cavity and deliver the specimen. Diseased perianal skin is excised, and the terminal ileum is divided with a linear stapler. The levators are closed with absorbable sutures, followed by additional sutures applied to the fat layer and skin.

The surgeon re-establishes pneumoperitoneum to confirm the correct orientation of the mesentery before maturing the stoma. A Blake drain is placed in the pelvis, and a final inspection confirms hemostasis and the correct orientation of the ileostomy, with a straight line visible through the cut edge of the small bowel mesentery down to the SMA origin.
